coded_segments.text: So in the mornings I don't really eat. I kind of skip breakfast. When I go to work, I go to work four hours. If it's an evening shift and I come home and I'm tired, I sometimes order a lot of food or if I'm not tired, I'll cook something small. But on the days where I do work in the morning I do try and cook myself. But the majority of the time recently I've just been ordering.

enrichment_tags.tension: Claims to cook but admits 'majority of the time recently I've just been ordering'

coded_segments.text: I would definitely say that I've been more open to trying different things, especially because I earn my own money. Back when I was a student, I was very much into budgeting, and because I only had a certain amount in my account, I had to spend it on transport, on uni appliances and all these things. I was kind of limited. But now that I earn my own money, I'm more accustomed to trying more restaurants, more different tastes. So it's definitely changed.

emergent_themes.description: How earning own money dramatically changes delivery behavior from budget-constrained to exploratory
TikTok and social media content directly driving delivery orders rather than cooking attempts
Relying on friends' negative experiences with subscriptions rather than trying personally
Frustration with promotions only available to new customers, driving platform switching

coding_summary: P024 reveals a participant in transition whose delivery behavior has dramatically shifted since earning her own money. Despite claiming to be a quality-focused cook, she admits to ordering 'the majority of the time recently.' Her behavior is heavily influenced by social media (15 hours daily), with TikTok content directly driving delivery orders. She actively switches between platforms for deals and is frustrated by new-customer-only promotions. Her subscription decisions are heavily influenced by peer experiences rather than personal trial.

coverage_gaps.reason: Participant is a recent graduate, no longer on campus - limited campus food data available
Lives with best friend in city center - accommodation type not extensively discussed
Participant orders frequently with increased income - barriers less relevant to current behavior
Primarily orders alone - limited group ordering context discussed
No evidence of stigma discussed - participant comfortable with frequent ordering
